course_description:
This course develops research skills and capabilities in qualitative research design, data collection and analysis techniques. Students will develop an understanding of qualitative research processes relevant to business questions and problems. Awareness of issues relating to data quality, coding practices, analysis and interpretation will be emphasised. The course learning focuses on students experiencing how to collect qualitative data, preparing it for analysis, and practicing interpretation using both traditional coding methods and new computer assisted data analysis tools such as Leximancer and Nvivo programs. Course assessments provide experience and practice in data collection, computer assisted data analysis software and presentation of qualitative insight. Students are encouraged to complete research assessment relevant to honours and/or doctoral research, or other projects of interest.

This course introduces students to qualitative research design, data collection and analysis techniques. Course readings and discussions will focus on qualitative research methods used regularly by business researchers, including widely used techniques such as interviewing, ethnography and observational methods, as well as Netnography to include assessment of new media data collection and analysis methods. Student learning in this course focuses on experiencing how to collect qualitative data, preparing it for analysis, and practicing interpretation using both traditional coding methods and new computer assisted data analysis tools such as Leximancer and Nvivo programs. Seminar discussions will emphasises issues relating to data quality, coding practices, analysis and interpretation.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

restrictions: Restricted to students in the BAdvBus(Hons), BBusMan(Hons), GCBA, GDipBRM, MBA, MEI, MPhil and PhD programs.  To enrol: BAdvBus(Hons) students must email bel@uq.edu.au; MBA students must email mba@business.uq.edu.au; all other students must email info@business.uq.edu.auQuota: Min. 10 enrolments.

course_aims: This course aims to introduce students with limited experience in qualitative research, to the basic concepts, methods and tool of qualitative social research. The course focuses on enabling students to experience the research process including learning to plan, execute and evaluate qualitative research. Students will be encouraged to develop research and complete assessment activities focused on topics relevant to their thesis research or other projects of interest.

learning_outcomes.description: Explain different qualitative methods and assess reported qualitative research results.

Understand how to gather and analyse data to present an argument based on qualitative research approaches that address issues of ethical practice, reflexivity, quality criteria and trustworthiness in research results.
Apply qualitative data analysis approaches to your area of business research (e.g., management, marketing, strategy).

Critically discuss the relevance and importance of qualitative methods in business research practice, and be able to communicate and justify your own approach to qualitative research in both written and spoken forms.

assessment_summary.title: Pilot Research Project
assessment_summary.due_date: Pilot Research Description - 2:00PM -  18/03/2026; Preliminary Coding Report - 2:00PM -  6/05/2026; Presentation of Pilot Findings - During Class -  Week 12 Wed - Week 13 Wed

assessment_details.title: Pilot Research Project
assessment_details.due_date: Pilot Research Description - 2:00PM -  18/03/2026Preliminary Coding Report - 2:00PM -  6/05/2026Presentation of Pilot Findings - During Class -  Week 12 Wed - Week 13 Wed
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment is designed as a progressive, hands-on inquiry sequence that develops students’ practical understanding of qualitative research. Part A builds foundational skills in research design and question alignment, Part B provides formative feedback on early coding and analytic reasoning, and Part C synthesises preliminary thematic analysis and methodological justification. Together, the components scaffold learning across design, analysis, and communication in qualitative inquiry.Part A: Pilot Research Description (10%)Students submit a short pilot research description outlining the purpose and goals of their study. This includes:an introduction and context for the research problem,a brief description of the conceptual or theoretical framework guiding the inquiry, anda rationale for a qualitative research design aligned with the student’s disciplinary foundations.This formative task provides early feedback on the clarity of research questions and the alignment between research aims, sampling strategy, and interview method.Due: Week 4 – Wednesday 18 March @ 2:00pmPart B: Preliminary Coding Report (20%)This formative assessment provides early feedback on students’ qualitative analysis prior to the final presentation. Students submit a formatted interview transcript, a short interview summary, preliminary coding of the transcript, a draft codebook (codes and definitions), and a coding memo outlining early patterns, analytic decisions, uncertainties, and next steps.Feedback focuses on the appropriateness of coding decisions, the coherence of code definitions, and the quality of evidence-based analytic reasoning.Due: Week 10 – Wednesday 6 May @ 2:00pmPart C: Presentation of Pilot Findings (40%)Building directly on the Preliminary Coding Report (Part B), students deliver an oral presentation reporting on their qualitative pilot project and developed preliminary analysis. The presentation must demonstrate progression from initial coding to early thematic patterns, with clear alignment between research questions, research design, analytic decisions, and supporting data. Students are also required to explain how quality criteria for qualitative research (e.g., credibility, dependability, reflexivity, transparency) have informed methodological and analytic choices across the inquiry process.Supporting documentation must be submitted on the presentation day and includes presentation slides, formatted and coded interview transcripts, data analysis tables (codes, definitions, and illustrative excerpts), and a two-page formative data analysis memo.Due: Presented in Weeks 12–13 during scheduled seminar sessionsPlease Note:The presentation will be recorded for marking purposes per UQ Policy.AI Statement:This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.Read full task descriptionHide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
See Turnitin submission links on the course Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able toapply for an extension.
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extensions or deferrals are not available for an in-class presentation. An extension may be available for the submitted material only.
Late submission
Apenaltyof 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
10% Late Penalty applies to submitted material only. Late submissions are not accepted for in-class presentations. Failure to present at the scheduled time will result in a mark of zero for the presentation portion of this assessment.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.
A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessment_details.title: Qualitative Research Paper
assessment_details.task_description: Purpose of the assessment:This assessment is designed to help students situate qualitative method choices within their discipline’s standards and to use that understanding to design a feasible, credible pilot study.Students will submit a 2,500-word research paper that critically reviews how qualitative research is conducted, justified, and evaluated within their discipline, drawing on a minimum of 10 influential peer-reviewed qualitative studies from high-quality journals. The paper must demonstrate disciplinary understanding of qualitative inquiry and include a considered discussion of how the qualitative methods planned for the student’s pilot study (and future research) align with disciplinary norms. An abstract of no more than 150 words must be included, along with a reference list, and students must consistently apply the referencing style customary to their discipline (e.g., APA).In this paper, students will:Explain how qualitative research is conducted in their disciplineDescribe how qualitative studies in the discipline typically collect and analyse data, including commonly used data collection methods and analytic approaches, and how these are justified in published research.Evaluate the strengths and limitations of qualitative inquiryAnalyse the types of research questions qualitative methods are well suited to in the discipline, as well as their recognised limitations or risks, drawing on disciplinary and social science methods literature.Identify contemporary challenges and opportunities in qualitative analysisSummarise key challenges and opportunities in qualitative data collection and analysis, including the use of qualitative data analysis software and emerging AI-supported tools, supported by appropriate scholarly sources.Explain ethics as a core feature of qualitative research practiceDescribe how ethical considerations shape qualitative research in the discipline, including participant protection, consent, confidentiality, power relations, and researcher positioning, and how these considerations inform the design of the student’s planned pilot study.AI Statement:This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.Read full task descriptionHide full task description
